title: Peace Talks Stalemate: Iran War Reaches 100 Days as Trump Rules Out Asset Unfreeze
subhead: Negotiations between the US and Iran remain deadlocked as the conflict reaches its 100th day. Pakistan's mediation push continues, but gaps over frozen assets and the Lebanon front remain unresolved. Meanwhile, Israel strikes Beirut despite a ceasefire deal, Taiwan expels Chinese vessels, and OPEC+ raises output quotas again.

sections.heading: US-Iran Peace Talks at Impasse
sections.body:
President Trump stated in an NBC interview aired Sunday that the US will only unfreeze Iranian assets after a peace deal is reached. 'No,' Trump responded when asked if he would release any Iranian assets or lift sanctions as part of an interim agreement. 'If they behave, if they do a good job, we start talking' about unfreezing assets, he added. The Hill reports that Iranian officials estimate more than $100 billion in assets are inaccessible due to sanctions, banking restrictions, and legal disputes. Iranian adviser Mohsen Rezaei told CNN on Friday that negotiations have hit an impasse over the Trump administration's refusal to release $24 billion in frozen Iranian assets. 'The negotiations are at a deadlock and Trump must break this deadlock,' Rezaei said. 'The ball is in Trump's court.'

Pakistan's Interior Minister Mohsin Naqvi met with Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi in Tehran on Sunday, delivering a message from Pakistan's army chief Field Marshal Asim Munir to Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei, according to Iran's state-run IRNA news agency. Pakistan continues to push for a diplomatic breakthrough with support from Qatar, Turkey, and Egypt. CNN reports that an Iranian spokesperson described US negotiating positions as 'contradictory.'
sections.whyItMatters: The 100-day mark underscores how entrenched the conflict has become. Iran wants $24 billion in frozen assets released as a precondition for any deal; the US wants a signed agreement first. With both sides waiting for the other to move first, the stalemate persists, and sporadic exchanges of fire continue testing the ceasefire.

sections.heading: US Plans to Redirect Iranian Assets for Gulf Reconstruction
sections.body:
Reuters reports that Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has directed a team to assess damage costs incurred by Gulf allies from Iranian strikes, with the US considering using frozen Iranian assets for reconstruction and repairs. The disclosure came as Iran launched drones and missiles at US bases in Kuwait and Bahrain. Kuwait's army said it engaged seven ballistic missiles that passed over residential areas, causing material damage but no casualties. In Bahrain, sirens sounded and residents were urged to seek shelter. The US military said six missiles were intercepted and a seventh did not reach its target.

According to Reuters, the language used to describe the new measures was not limited to frozen assets. The threat to redirect Iranian assets could create a new irritant to the fragile ceasefire, as Tehran views this as effectively confiscating Iranian sovereign wealth for purposes Iran did not consent to.
sections.whyItMatters: The US move to potentially redirect Iranian assets for Gulf reconstruction signals a harder line: Washington is treating frozen assets as leverage and potential reparations, not just bargaining chips. This reduces Iran's incentive to negotiate and raises the stakes for any diplomatic breakthrough.

sections.heading: Israel Strikes Beirut Despite Ceasefire Agreement
sections.body:
AP News reports that Israel struck Beirut's southern suburbs on Sunday, days after a US-brokered ceasefire agreement went into effect. Lebanon's state-run news agency said two people were killed and 11 wounded. An unexploded weapon was found in the rubble. Israel said the strikes targeted Hezbollah 'command centers' in retaliation for rockets fired at northern Israel. Hezbollah did not claim responsibility for firing at Israel.

Iran had warned that an attack on Beirut would renew full-scale war across the Middle East. AP reports that Netanyahu told his Cabinet that 'Hezbollah is on the run' and wants to press ahead with Israel's offensive until Hezbollah no longer poses a threat. Trump, in his NBC interview, said he would like to see 'a more surgical attack on Hezbollah.' More than 3,500 people have been killed in Lebanon since the war began March 2, and over 1 million have been displaced.
sections.whyItMatters: The Beirut strike tests the US-brokered ceasefire and demonstrates that Israel will continue operations against Hezbollah regardless of diplomatic timelines. Iran wants any US-Iran deal to include ending the Lebanon war; Netanyahu wants to remove Hezbollah as a threat entirely. These conflicting aims make a comprehensive deal harder.

sections.heading: Taiwan Expels Chinese Coast Guard Vessels
sections.body:
Reuters reports that Taiwan's coast guard 'expelled' four Chinese government ships that entered restricted waters off the island's south on Sunday. The two sides broadcast testy warnings to each other, in an escalation of tensions. Taiwan's coast guard said the Chinese vessels entered waters near the Pratas Islands, a strategically important location in the South China Sea. MSN reports that China's transport ministry announced what it called a 'special law enforcement operation' near Taiwan on Saturday, raising questions about whether a larger campaign is underway.

The incident comes amid broader tensions in the Taiwan Strait and South China Sea, with Taiwan accusing China of coordinated provocations near key islands.
sections.whyItMatters: While global attention focuses on the Iran conflict, China continues probing operations around Taiwan. These incidents test Taiwan's response capabilities and establish new operational patterns. The more China normalizes such operations, the harder it becomes to deter future escalation.

sections.heading: Ukraine Turns to Europe as US Steps Back from Mediation
sections.body: The New York Times reports that President Volodymyr Zelensky traveled to London to meet with the leaders of Britain, Germany, and France, whom he said could help resolve the conflict with Russia. The shift comes as the US scales back its role as mediator in Ukraine peace talks. Ukraine is seeking alternative diplomatic channels as American attention and resources remain focused on the Iran conflict.
sections.whyItMatters: The Ukraine war has become a secondary priority for US diplomacy as the Iran conflict consumes bandwidth. European leaders are now the primary interlocutors for Kyiv. This shift may accelerate European strategic autonomy or leave Ukraine with fewer options if US support continues to decline.

sections.heading: OPEC+ Approves Fourth Straight Output Hike
sections.body:
The Financial Express reports that OPEC+ approved a production increase of 188,000 barrels per day for July, marking the fourth consecutive monthly output hike. The decision involves seven participating countries: Saudi Arabia, Russia, Iraq, Kuwait, Kazakhstan, Algeria, and Oman. However, higher quotas do not translate to higher production. OPEC+ production averaged 33.19 million bpd in April, compared to 42.77 million bpd in February. The gap between official targets and actual output has widened as the Strait of Hormuz disruption limits exports from key Gulf producers.

Reuters notes that the United Arab Emirates left OPEC after nearly six decades of membership, removing one of the group's major oil producers from the organization and complicating output calculations.
sections.whyItMatters: OPEC+ is raising targets on paper but cannot deliver the oil. The gap between quotas and actual production reveals how severely the Hormuz closure has disrupted global energy markets. Higher quotas signal intent to stabilize prices, but markets understand the physical constraints remain.

bottomLine: The Iran-US conflict has reached a symbolic milestone at 100 days with no resolution in sight. Trump has ruled out unfreezing Iranian assets before a deal, while Iran demands $24 billion in released funds as a precondition. Pakistan continues mediation efforts but both sides appear dug in. The US is now planning to redirect frozen Iranian assets toward Gulf reconstruction, a move Tehran will view as confiscation. Israel's strike on Beirut demonstrates that the Lebanon front remains volatile despite ceasefire agreements. Meanwhile, China probes Taiwan's waters, Ukraine turns to Europe as US attention drifts, and OPEC+ raises quotas it cannot fill. The global system is absorbing multiple shocks simultaneously with no clear path to resolution on any front.
